title: Climbing Stairs
description: You are climbing a staircase with `n` steps. Each time you can climb either 1 or 2 steps. Return the number of distinct ways you can climb to the top.
hints: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2) — classic Fibonacci recurrence.
Base: ways(0)=1, ways(1)=1.
Two variables enough — O(1) space.

title: Climbing Stairs With K Steps
description: You are climbing a staircase with `n` steps. At each move you may climb any number of steps from 1 up to `k`. Starting from the ground (step 0), return the number of distinct ways to reach the top (step `n`).
constraints: 1 <= k <= n or k = 1 when n = 0
hints: Use a running window sum to avoid the inner k-loop — O(n).
Base dp[0] = 1.

title: Climbing Stairs Without Consecutive Double Steps
description: You are climbing a staircase with `n` steps, taking either 1 or 2 steps at a time. You are not allowed to take two 2-steps in a row (the previous move and the current move cannot both be 2). Return the number of distinct climbing sequences that obey this rule.

examples.explanation: 1111, 112, 121, 211, 22 is BANNED (two 2s consecutively allowed only with one between), wait re-check. Sequences: 1111, 112, 121, 211, 22 — 22 has the only two-step pair adjacent so it's banned; valid = 4? Actually rule says two consecutive 2-steps not allowed, 22 has them adjacent, so it's banned; the count is 4.

hints: State: dp[i][last] where last ∈ {1,2} indicates last step size.
Transitions: from last=1 you can step 1 or 2; from last=2 you can only step 1.
Roll into two scalars for O(1) space.

title: House Robber
description: You are given an integer array `nums` representing values arranged in a row. Select a subset of elements to maximize their total sum, subject to the constraint that you may not pick two adjacent elements. Return the maximum total you can collect.
hints: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]) — skip or take.
Base dp[0]=nums[0], dp[1]=max(nums[0],nums[1]).
Two scalars → O(1) space.

title: Coin Change
description: You are given an integer array `coins` of coin denominations and an integer `amount`. Return the minimum number of coins needed to make up exactly `amount`. You may use each denomination an unlimited number of times. If `amount` cannot be formed by any combination of the coins, return `-1`.
hints: dp[a] = min over c in coins of (dp[a-c] + 1).
Initialise dp[0]=0, others = Infinity.
Skip coins larger than current amount.

title: Coin Change II
description: You are given an integer array `plans` of distinct denominations and an integer `amount`. Return the number of distinct combinations of denominations (each usable an unlimited number of times) that sum to exactly `amount`. Order does not matter, so two selections using the same denominations in a different order count as one combination.
hints: Outer loop on coins, inner on amount — order matters here!
dp[a] += dp[a - coin].
Outer-on-amount would count permutations instead.

title: Word Break
description: Given a string `s` and a list of strings `words`, return `true` if `s` can be segmented into a sequence of one or more strings from `words`, and `false` otherwise. The same word from `words` may be reused any number of times in the segmentation.
hints: dp[i] = true if s[0..i-1] can be segmented.
For each i, look at each j < i: if dp[j] && s.substring(j,i) in dict, set dp[i]=true.
Use a Set for O(1) word lookup.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Given an integer array `ratings`, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence. A subsequence is formed by deleting zero or more elements without changing the relative order of the remaining elements.

examples.explanation: 1,4,5,9 length 4 or 1,4,5,6 length 4? Actual LIS: 1,4,5,9 → 4. Let me re-check: 3,4,5,9 → 4; 1,4,5,9 → 4; 1,4,5,6 → 4; longest is 4. Output 4.

hints: O(n^2) DP: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) for j<i with ratings[j]<ratings[i].
O(n log n) patience sort: maintain tails array; binary search insertion point.
Strict increasing → use lower_bound, replace at insertion point.

title: Maximum Subarray
description: Given an integer array `nums`, find the contiguous subarray (containing at least one element) with the largest sum and return that sum.
hints: Kadane: cur = max(num, cur + num); best = max(best, cur).
Initialise both with nums[0].
All-negative case: best is the largest single element.

title: Decode Ways
description: A message containing letters A-Z is encoded using the mapping 'A' → 1, 'B' → 2, ..., 'Z' → 26. Given a string `s` consisting of digits, return the number of ways to decode it. A '0' has no valid single-digit mapping, and a two-digit group is valid only if it lies between 10 and 26.
hints: dp[i] = ways to decode prefix of length i.

title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
description: Given an array of positive integers `weights`, return `true` if the array can be partitioned into two subsets whose sums are equal, and `false` otherwise.
hints: Total odd → return false.
Subset-sum DP: bool dp[s] = can we hit sum s?
Iterate weights; for each, update dp from high to low to keep 0/1 knapsack semantics.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
description: You are given an integer array `prices` where `prices[i]` is the price of an asset on day `i`. You may buy on one day and sell on a strictly later day. Return the maximum profit you can achieve. If no profit is possible, return 0.
hints: Track running min so far; for each day compute price - minSoFar.
Update best profit.
Single pass O(n), O(1) space.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
description: You are given an integer array `prices` where `prices[i]` is the price on day `i`. You may complete as many buy-sell transactions as you like, but you can hold at most one unit at a time, and after you sell you must wait one day (cooldown) before buying again. Return the maximum profit you can achieve.
hints: Three states per day: hold, sold (just sold today, must cooldown next), rest (idle, free to buy).
Transitions: hold = max(prevHold, prevRest - price); sold = prevHold + price; rest = max(prevRest, prevSold).
Roll into 3 scalars.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Transaction Fee
description: You are given an integer array `prices` where `prices[i]` is the price on day `i`, and an integer `fee` charged on each transaction. You may complete as many buy-sell transactions as you like but may hold at most one unit at a time. Return the maximum profit achievable after paying `fee` on every sale.
hints: Two states: hold, cash.
cash = max(cash, hold + price - fee).
hold = max(hold, cash - price).

title: Jump Game
description: You are given an integer array `nums`. You start at index 0, and `nums[i]` is the maximum jump length from index `i`, meaning you can jump to any index in the range `[i+1, i + nums[i]]`. Return `true` if you can reach the last index, and `false` otherwise.
hints: Greedy: track furthest reachable index so far.
If i > maxReach, you're stuck. Otherwise update maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]).
DP: dp[i] = true if reachable.

title: Jump Game II
description: You are given an integer array `nums` and start at index 0. From index `i` you can jump forward at most `nums[i]` steps. Return the minimum number of jumps needed to reach the last index. The last index is guaranteed to be reachable.
hints: Greedy/BFS-like: track current jump's farthest, when you exhaust it increment jumps and update farthest.
currentEnd, farthest variables.
DP O(n^2): dp[i] = min over reachable j of dp[j]+1.

title: Maximum Product Subarray
description: Given an integer array `nums`, find the contiguous subarray (containing at least one element) that has the largest product and return that product.
hints: Negative * negative = positive: track BOTH max and min ending at i.
On hitting a negative, swap roles before computing.
Reset when 0 (effectively starts fresh).

title: Perfect Squares
description: Given an integer `n`, return the minimum number of perfect squares (1, 4, 9, 16, ...) that sum to `n`. The same perfect square may be used more than once.
hints: dp[i] = min over k where k*k <= i of dp[i - k*k] + 1.
Initialise dp[0]=0.
Lagrange's four-square theorem: answer is always <= 4.

title: Integer Break
description: Given an integer `n` with `n >= 2`, break it into a sum of at least two positive integers and maximize the product of those integers. Return the maximum product you can obtain.
hints: dp[i] = max over j in 1..i-1 of max(j*(i-j), j*dp[i-j]).
Base dp[2]=1.
Math: prefer breaking into 3s once n >= 4.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock IV
description: You are given an integer `k` and an integer array `prices` where `prices[i]` is the price on day `i`. You may complete at most `k` buy-sell transactions, holding at most one unit at a time and starting a new transaction only after the previous one is closed. Return the maximum profit you can achieve.
hints: buy[j] = max(buy[j], sell[j-1] - price); sell[j] = max(sell[j], buy[j] + price).
Iterate prices outer, j from 1..k inner.
If k >= n/2, behaves like unlimited transactions.

title: House Robber II
description: Given an integer array `nums` arranged in a circle, where the first and last elements are adjacent, return the maximum sum of a selected subset such that no two adjacent elements are chosen. Because the layout is circular, the first and last elements cannot both be selected.

examples.explanation: Cannot take both 0 and 2; pick 3 alone.

hints: Run linear robber twice: once excluding first index, once excluding last index.
Return the max of the two.
Edge case: n=1 → nums[0].

title: Paint House
description: You are given an `n x 3` integer matrix `costs`, where `costs[i]` is a 3-element array giving the cost of painting item `i` with color 0, color 1, or color 2. No two adjacent items may be painted the same color. Return the minimum total cost to paint all items.
hints: Three running scalars for the 3 colors.
newRed = costs[i][0] + min(prevBlue, prevGreen). Similarly for others.
Final answer = min of three at end.

title: Maximum Sum of Non-Adjacent Elements
description: Given an integer array `offers` of non-negative values, return the maximum sum obtainable by selecting elements such that no two selected elements are adjacent.
hints: Same as house robber.
dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + offers[i]).
O(1) space with two scalars.

title: Minimum Jumps With Fixed Step Sizes
description: You start at index 0 on a path of `n` cells indexed from 0 to `n-1`. At each move you may jump forward by exactly one of the step sizes given in `allowed`. Return the minimum number of jumps needed to land exactly on index `n-1`. If it cannot be reached, return `-1`.

examples.explanation: Cannot land on 3 from 0 with only step 2.

hints: BFS-like DP: dp[i] = min jumps to reach i.
dp[i] = min over s in allowed of dp[i-s] + 1, if dp[i-s] valid.
Initialise dp[0]=0, others = Infinity.

title: Decode Ways II
description: A message of letters A-Z is encoded using 'A' → 1, 'B' → 2, ..., 'Z' → 26. The string `s` may also contain the character '*', which can represent any single digit from 1 to 9. Return the number of ways to decode `s`, modulo 10^9 + 7. As in standard decoding, a '0' has no valid single-digit mapping and any two-digit group must lie between 10 and 26.

examples.explanation: * as 0..9 single = 9 if non-zero each, plus pair 10..19 = 9 more, but pair 10 is 1 valid, etc. Actual count: 18.

hints: Same DP as decode ways but each digit position has multiple possibilities.
Single decode contributions: '*' gives 9, '0' gives 0, else 1.
Two-digit decode contributions: enumerate pairs (* with digit, digit with *, ** etc.).

title: Number of Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Given an integer array `nums`, return the number of longest strictly increasing subsequences. You must return the count of such subsequences, not the subsequences themselves.
hints: Two arrays: len[i] = LIS length ending at i; cnt[i] = number of such LISes.
For each j < i with nums[j] < nums[i]: if len[j]+1 > len[i], reset; if equal, accumulate.
Sum cnt[i] over all i with len[i] == max len.

title: Minimum Deletions to Make Array Increasing
description: Given an integer array `nums`, return the minimum number of elements you must delete so that the remaining elements, kept in their original order, form a strictly increasing sequence.

examples.explanation: LIS length is 4 (1,4,5,9 or similar), so deletions = 8-4 = 4.

hints: Answer = n - LIS(nums) (strict).
Use O(n log n) patience-sort LIS.
Strict: lower_bound on tails.
